sections.title: Shapes in rangoli
sections.prose_summary: The chapter opens with a rangoli context that draws children into noticing and naming 2D shapes — circles, squares, triangles, and rectangles — present in a decorative pattern. Students work at the concrete level by drawing the rangoli on a dot grid and using shape cut-outs to create their own designs. The section sits firmly on the concrete path, with shape identification anchored to a culturally familiar visual stimulus.

tasks.description: Make Amma's rangoli on the dots given below.
Name the shapes drawn in Amma's rangoli: _____, _____, _____
How many shapes are made with (a) Curved lines (b) Straight lines
Use cut outs of shapes to make a rangoli design. Outline the object and colour.
Try to make the following objects using shape cutouts.

sections.title: Shapes from boxes — cuboid faces
sections.prose_summary: This section introduces the idea that 3D objects (cuboids) have 2D faces by having children physically open cardboard boxes and trace the faces. Students discover that the faces of a cuboid are rectangles or squares, and that different faces of the same object can yield different rectangle sizes. The section moves from concrete handling (opening/tracing physical objects) toward early reasoning about 3D–2D relationships.

tasks.description: Collect some cardboard boxes and open them up carefully. What shapes do you see in the flattened boxes?
Make an Envelope. Use a square piece of paper and fold it as shown in the picture.
Name any three objects that have rectangular faces.
Trace all the faces of any cuboidal object (example—sharpener or eraser). Answer (a)–(f) about faces, shapes, squares, and triangles.

sections.title: Rectangles — properties and drawing
sections.prose_summary: Students build understanding of rectangles through construction on dot grids: completing rectangles from given sides, drawing larger rectangles around a given one, and counting rectangles embedded in a figure. The section then formalises properties — 4 sides, 4 corners, opposite sides equal — by having students observe a set of differently-oriented rectangles and describe what they notice. The path is concrete–pictorial–symbolic, ending with student-generated descriptions.

tasks.description: Construct the rectangles using the sides given below.
Draw 3 bigger rectangles around the given rectangle.
Count and write the number of rectangles in the following picture.
Look at the different rectangles given below: (a) How many sides? (b) How many corners? (c) Equal sides? (d) Describe in your own words.

sections.title: Same to Same — squares vs rectangles
sections.prose_summary: The section uses a board game context (chess and ludo boards, tiles) to introduce the square and contrast it with the rectangle. Students discover that a square has all four sides equal, while a rectangle only needs opposite sides to be equal. Hands-on work with matchsticks, dot-grid drawing, and square cut-outs deepens understanding of the square as a special case of the rectangle, and extends to composing new shapes from square units.

tasks.description: How many squares do you see in this drawing? Both have ___ sides. Both have ___ corners.
Here is a square. Draw 2 bigger squares around this square.
Use matchsticks to make a square so that it has squares on all its sides. How many squares did you get?
Complete the squares using the sides given below.
Use the square cutouts: How many different shapes can you make by joining (a) 2 squares (b) 3 squares (c) 4 squares. Show them in a dot grid.
Tick the shapes that are rectangles. Which figures are not rectangles? Explain why.
Can you fold all the corners of a square sheet so that the number of corners remains the same?
Make a square on cardboard, cut along dotted lines to get 4 triangles. Make as many different shapes as possible by joining three/four triangles.

sections.title: Square corners — right angles
sections.prose_summary: Students physically handle paper strips joined at a pivot to make and compare three types of corners: less than, equal to, and more than a square corner (introducing the intuitive idea of a right angle). They use the strip-tester on real objects and on dot grids to identify, mark, and create square corners. Matchstick tasks extend to counting and reasoning about square corners in composite figures.

tasks.description: Are the corners of a square the same? Are corners of square and rectangle the same? Name objects in class that have only square corners.
Mark the square corners in these shapes.
Connect the dots to make some squares. How many different squares did you get?
Look at the picture: (a) Count and write the number of corners. (b) Circle the square corners.
Use two matchsticks to make two square corners and then four square corners. Draw and show it in the space given below.
Murugan made three squares with 10 matchsticks. How many squares can you make with 12 matchsticks?

sections.title: Triangles — three sides, three corners
sections.prose_summary: The triangle is introduced through culturally familiar objects with triangular faces (rangoli patterns, flags, food). Students observe that all triangles have 3 sides and 3 corners, then count triangles in rangoli figures, reason about how many triangles can be formed from dots on a circle, and use matchsticks to transform one triangle into two. The section stays on the concrete–pictorial path with strong exploratory and reasoning tasks.

tasks.description: Draw and name some triangular objects that you see around yourself, in your notebook.
Count the number of triangles in the given rangoli. (a) and (b)
How many different triangles can be made using the dots on this circle?
Move two matchsticks to turn the one triangle into two triangles.

sections.title: Circus with circles — circle and centre
sections.prose_summary: Circles are introduced through a circus scene and everyday objects (bangles, bottle caps, rings). Students trace circles using physical objects and then fold a paper plate in halves to discover that folds through the circle all pass through one point — the centre — and that the longest fold line (diameter) is longer than others. This moves from concrete handling to an early pictorial understanding of the circle's properties.

tasks.description: Name some objects that are like circles.
Draw colourful circles to complete the circus scene.
Draw circles by tracing bottle caps, bangles, and rings in your notebook.
Try to make a circle on the playground.

sections.title: Comparing and composing shapes
sections.prose_summary: The final section brings together all shapes studied — triangles, rectangles, squares, circles, and other polygons — in tasks that require comparison, decomposition, composition, and pattern extension. Students compare pairs of shapes for similarities and differences, find embedded rectangles, extend triangle-row patterns, count squares torn from rectangles, classify odd-one-out shapes, fill rectangle gaps, split shapes into triangles, reproduce shapes in multiple orientations, and create patterns using the Tangram and line work. This is the most cognitively varied section, consolidating the whole chapter.

tasks.description: Look at these two shapes (square and triangle): discuss similarities and differences. Tick (a) corners: same/different (b) number of sides: same/different.
Choose any pair of shapes. Share the similarities and differences in these shapes with your friends.
Find the largest rectangle in these shapes.
I made one triangle, then another row. How many triangles in second figure? If one more row, how many in the third figure?
Here are some rectangles that are torn. How many square pieces have been torn from each shape?
Each of these shapes can be the odd one out. How is each one odd? Discuss.
To complete the rectangle, tick the appropriate shapes from the left side to fill the gaps in the shape on the right side.
Draw two lines to split the shape into three triangles.
Draw one line to split the shape into 3 triangles.
Make the following shapes with different sizes and orientations in your notebook: (a) Triangle (b) Rectangle (c) Circle (d) Any other shape of your choice.
Try to make your own border designs using both curved and straight lines.
Continue the following line pattern.
Use the pieces from the tangram puzzle given in the end of the book. Can you create these shapes using some of the pieces?
